aliases: Indonesian Mixed Vegetable Salad with Peanut Sauce

english: Indonesian Mixed Vegetable Salad with Peanut Sauce

ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dish — mixed vegetable salad with legume-based sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for gado-gado; A0FKP (composite vegetable dish) is the closest defensible analogous anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for gado-gado as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of gado-gado is approximately 300 g, comprising a mixed plate of boiled vegetables, tofu, tempeh, lontong, a hard-boiled egg, kerupuk, and a generous ladle of peanut sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Gado-Gado — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Gado-Gado. All per-100g values are preserved from the input record which carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 18 g/100g based on the known coconut-milk-heavy peanut sauce composition; this figure is consistent with the input health_suitability notes and is flagged as an estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for gado-gado exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated using the closest analogous composite: mixed vegetable dish with rice (lontong) and legume-based sauce. Lontong (compressed rice cake) is the primary carbohydrate driver; white rice GI is approximately 64–72 (University of Sydney GI Database). The peanut sauce and vegetables substantially lower the overall meal GI through fat, protein, and fibre buffering. A blended GI of 55 is defensible for this composite. Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), GI 55 falls in the low category. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 5.9 × 3.0) / 100 = 9.7 g per 300 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100 g — critically exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit gado-gado due to sodium at 810 mg/100 g; if consumed, request peanut sauce on the side and avoid additional kecap manis or soy sauce to minimise sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High fat (44.5 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~18 g/100 g from coconut-milk peanut sauce worsens insulin resistance; lontong adds high-GI refined carbohydrate; dietary fibre is 0 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request reduced peanut sauce to lower saturated fat, omit or substitute lontong with additional non-starchy vegetables to reduce glycaemic load, and request reduced-sugar sauce to minimise palm sugar contribution.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~18 g/100 g from coconut milk in peanut sauce raises LDL cholesterol; sodium 810 mg/100 g elevates blood pressure risk; cholesterol 26 mg/100 g contributes additional cardiovascular burden
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit gado-gado consumption due to high saturated fat from coconut-milk peanut sauce and critically elevated sodium; request a reduced-coconut-milk sauce and consume as an occasional dish only.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~18 g/100 g triggers pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); sodium 810 mg/100 g worsens RA-related tissue swelling; potato and chili are nightshades that may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request a reduced or coconut-milk-free peanut sauce, consider substituting potato with additional non-nightshade vegetables, and request reduced chili if nightshade-sensitive.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gado-Gado is a traditional Indonesian mixed vegetable salad dressed with a rich, savoury peanut sauce. The dish typically comprises boiled or steamed vegetables including cabbage, long beans, cucumber, and potato, combined with protein components such as hard-boiled egg, fried tofu, and tempeh, alongside lontong (compressed rice cake) and kerupuk (prawn crackers). The peanut sauce is made from ground roasted peanuts, coconut milk, palm sugar, chili, garlic, and kecap manis (sweet soy sauce), giving it a thick, creamy, sweet-savoury-spicy flavour profile. The dish is widely consumed across Indonesia and is popular in Singapore's hawker centres and Indonesian restaurants. Nutritionally, gado-gado is calorie-dense at 430 kcal/100 g, driven primarily by the high fat content of 44.5 g/100 g from the coconut-milk peanut sauce. Sodium is critically elevated at 810 mg/100 g, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension. Estimated saturated fat of approximately 18 g/100 g from coconut milk raises cardiovascular and insulin-resistance concerns. Carbohydrate content is moderate at 5.9 g/100 g, primarily from lontong and palm sugar. Dietary fibre is reported as 0 g/100 g in the HPB source, which is atypically low for a vegetable-based dish and may reflect the sauce-dominant composition of the analysed sample. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker contexts and is dairy-free but contains major allergens including peanuts, egg, soy, and shellfish (from kerupuk).
common_user_queries: What is in gado-gado peanut sauce?
Gado-gado vs rojak — what is the difference?
Can I eat gado-gado if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 with canonical provenance. Saturated fat is estimated from known composition of coconut-milk peanut sauce and is flagged accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate documented with approved-source basis.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 g/100 g per the HPB source. This is unusually low for a vegetable-based dish and likely reflects the sauce-dominant composition of the analysed sample or a measurement artefact; it is preserved as reported and flagged for review. Confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. 2. saturated_fat_g (18.0 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from the known coconut-milk composition of the peanut sauce and is consistent with the input health_suitability notes; it is not directly reported in the HPB source. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this gado-gado preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for gado-gado exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), GI 55 falls in the low category; gi_category is set to 'low' accordingly. 5. FoodEx2 code A0FKP is an analogous anchor; no exact FoodEx2 code for gado-gado exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
